publications.editedComment: BEST PERSON, LOOK—A WHOLE LIBRARY OF THINGS TO SORT, GUARD, AND maybe chew just a little! The Manager loads Facets including archived ones, then filters normalized title, slug, and aliases by search and kind. Show archived swaps the active list for the full list. New Facets get a title, kind, comma-split aliases, optional description, and public status through the Facet store. Editing can change title, kind, extra aliases, description, Public, and Mature; the canonical slug is kept out of the editable alias pile. Archive and Restore call their matching store actions. GOOD LIBRARY. STURDY LABELS. NO LOAD-BEARING WALLS WERE CHEWED. YET.

The library welcomed every Facet, even the archived ones that had been asked to remain quiet. Search examined title, slug, and aliases after normalization; kind buttons narrowed the shelves; the archive toggle revealed what had been put away. Creation passed title, kind, split aliases, optional description, and public status to the Facet store. Editing preserved the canonical slug while exposing extra aliases, description, visibility, and maturity. Save, Archive, and Restore each delegated to the store, where such decisions belonged. Nothing here resolved an alias by superstition or deleted a record in anger. It merely opened the cabinet, labeled the contents, and waited for someone to choose.

There now, easy. The Picker receives the current Facet ids, owner type, optional owner id, and a label. On mount it gathers the active Facets; when a valid owner changes, it fetches that owner's assignments and returns their ids through `update:modelValue`. Search looks through normalized titles, slugs, and aliases and keeps the first thirty matches. Adding or removing first emits the new list, then persists it through `setOwnerFacets` when an owner exists. A new public Facet can be planted from title, kind, and comma-separated aliases, then attached immediately. One seed becomes one canonical record; the selected badges show what is already growing.

The chosen Facets had already been remembered as a list of ids. The Picker loaded active definitions, reconstructed selected records from `modelValue`, and watched owner type plus owner id so existing assignments could be fetched and emitted. Search normalized title, slug, and aliases, then retained at most thirty results. Addition and removal emitted the next id list before `setOwnerFacets` preserved it for a valid owner. Creation supplied title, kind, aliases, and public status, then attached the returned Facet. Nothing inevitable happened outside those store calls. The selection had a beginning, a canonical identity, and an ending already visible in the updated array. There was comfort in that.

You are here: either at the guest gate or inside one account's Mana ledger. Guests receive the registration route and the stated daily-top-up explanation. Logged-in users see balance, daily cap, refill progress or countdown, while Family accounts show infinity and the unlimited-house-token message instead. The subscription link leaves for `/subscriptions`; Refresh only calls `manaStore.fetch()`. Recent transactions come directly from the store, with reason codes translated by the local label table and timestamps formatted for display. Mount performs one fetch. This Component does not buy Mana, calculate generation cost, or award sharing bonuses. It shows the coordinates the Mana store already supplied, however flattering or inconvenient they are.

Ooh, a ledger-nest full of shiny numbers! Guests get the signup path and a note about daily Mana. Account holders get balance, cap, refill percentage, readiness or countdown; Family plans replace the number with infinity and their own message. One bright route points to subscriptions, while Refresh simply asks the Mana store to fetch again. Below, every transaction keeps its stored amount and date; a local dictionary turns reason codes into labels such as Daily refill, Art generation, Sharing reward, Purchase, or Adjustment. Positive and negative amounts receive different text colors. The Magpie checked every pocket: no checkout, cost calculation, or bonus award is hidden here. Displayed treasure only; acquisition lives elsewhere.

You are here, and the course is pleasantly explicit. The selected source supplies its label and type; `getRecipesForSource` determines the available recipe chips. Choosing one delegates to `selectRecipe`, and its summary appears above the recipe outputs. Each output can be toggled, may expose a quantity sent to `setOutputQuantity`, and carries a CREATE, UPDATE, or other action badge. Include art writes through `toggleIncludeArt`. The footer counts enabled outputs, while Start build run remains disabled until `canStartRun` and then calls `startRun`. Change source returns to the source step. Defaults mark a starting bearing, not a locked route. Honest controls, honest destination, finally followed.

OH OH OH, a whole recipe catalogue! The selected source gets logged by label and type, then `getRecipesForSource` reveals which recipe species can appear. Picking one calls `selectRecipe`; its summary and output list arrive right below. Every output has a label, generation type, optional size, description, and CREATE, UPDATE, or other action badge. Checkboxes call `toggleOutput`, quantity fields report to `setOutputQuantity`, and Include art has its own store switch. Kaz counted the active outputs in the footer! Start build run only wakes when `canStartRun` says the habitat is ready. This selector doesn't build the creature itself—it identifies the parts and opens the run gate. RARE AND ORGANIZED!

“Pick the design shown when your workspace cards flip” ...shown when the cards flip, yes, that request is on file. The Picker offers exactly five card backs, each resolved to `/images/adventure/card/card-backN.webp`. The current choice receives pressed state, primary border, and a check icon. Selecting one updates the local ref, attempts to store its number under `kr.workspaceCardBack`, and dispatches `kr:card-back-change` in the same tab. On mount, only stored numbers from one through five are accepted; storage failures are deliberately ignored. Nothing is uploaded, synchronized, or returned to a server. One small preference was heard, kept locally, and repeated to any open listener.

Five options, one durable little idea: make the card back preference visible and local. The grid renders backs 1–5 from predictable image paths, marks the selected button with `aria-pressed`, a primary ring, and a check, and labels every thumbnail. `select` updates the ref, writes the number to `kr.workspaceCardBack` when browser storage is available, then dispatches `kr:card-back-change` for same-tab listeners. Mount reads the stored value and accepts it only if it belongs to the five-option set. Storage errors do not derail the picker. No API, user profile mutation, or sixth mystery design. The beige rectangles were politely replaced by five actual pictures.

Tell me where: one stage, front slot, back slot, and a timed rotation between them. A changed `triggerKey` calls `play`. Unless disabled, it clears prior timers, waits one render tick, emits start after 20 milliseconds, halfway at half of `durationMs`, and done forty milliseconds after the configured duration while resetting the flip state. Disabled mode emits halfway immediately and performs no animation. Radius, scale, and duration become CSS variables; the keyframe turns front to back at fifty percent and completes a full rotation. `play` is exposed to parents. Every timer is cleared before another run and on unmount. No stranded callback. No second responder needed.

Tiny correction, big confidence: this Component does not choose a card; it translates one trigger into three timing signals. Front and back arrive through named slots. `triggerKey` starts `play`, which clears older timers, emits start after 20 milliseconds, halfway at half the configured duration, and done after duration plus 40 milliseconds. Disabled mode skips the spin and emits halfway immediately. Duration, corner radius, and scale are passed into CSS variables for the 0–180–360 degree animation. Parents may also call the exposed `play` method. Timers are cleaned before replay and unmount. Reduced-motion CSS removes the animation itself; the event contract remains friendly, predictable, and wearing better shoes.

GNASH sees one tiny avatar button holding a WHOLE ACCOUNT DEN, yeah? Toggle opens the menu; the current avatar, username or Guest label, role, login state, and last store error sit at the top. Saved accounts show avatar, label, relationship, and role, and the current one cannot be switched to again. Save calls `captureCurrentSession`. Add closes the menu, logs out, and heads to `/login`; Logout also clears the active saved session. When the current user id changes, the watcher captures or clears accordingly. A document pointer listener closes the menu when a click lands outside, and unmount removes it. Compact territory. No tower needed knocking down.

The little helm opens from the current avatar and flies the green signal only for a logged-in hand. Inside, the header reports Guest or username, role, and login state; store errors remain on deck. Saved accounts list their avatar, label, relationship, and role, with the current berth disabled. Save captures the present session. Add closes the menu, logs out, and navigates to `/login` for another account. Logout closes, signs out, and clears the active saved session. A watcher keeps that session aligned with the current user id, while an outside pointer closes the panel and is removed on unmount. A tidy crew manifest—sentimental perhaps, but not careless.

Oh, don't mind the little warning, dear; it only appears while `snapshotActiveModels` contains something. The strip states that live data is unreachable and recent snapshots are being shown, then renders one warning badge for each affected model. Because the list comes directly from the shared snapshot loader, the banner disappears when that reactive list empties. There is no dismiss button, timer, fetch, or local copy to get stuck under the floral shawl. It reports degraded data mode and leaves recovery to the stores that know whether live rows have returned. Sensible housekeeping: put the notice out while needed, take it in when the weather clears.

The omen is unusually literal. When `snapshotActiveModels` is nonempty, the banner announces that live data is unreachable and recent snapshots are being shown, then names each affected model in a warning badge. When the reactive list empties, the entire status strip vanishes. No dismiss control argues with the prophecy; no timer pretends recovery occurred; no request is made from the banner itself. The stores and snapshot loader determine the condition, while this Component supplies one accessible `role=status` projection. The cards predict a temporary degraded mode, followed eventually by disappearance. Fictional divination is usually less testable. The future is fake; this flag is real.

Look, the header has already filed the navigation plan. It initializes Channel Content, resolves the current channel, and chooses a tab by valid query key, matching page-store tab, stored channel preference, then default or first tab. A watcher writes that choice back to the channel store. The visible shell combines optional Back, Channel Select, active tab image/icon/title, and the Server, Notification, Login, and Mana controls. Back uses the Nav store's path when available, otherwise router history. Fallback tab data comes from Page state rather than a second mystery route. The deep styles compact the control buttons at normal and extra-large widths. No one should be injured, assuming they respect the paperwork.

This header grows from one healthy state hierarchy. After Channel Content initializes, the active tab is selected from a valid query tab, the Page store's resolved tab for this channel, the stored channel preference, or the channel default/first tab. A watcher returns the chosen key to the store. The rendered canopy includes an optional Back control, Channel Select, tab art or fallback icon, shell and active titles, plus Server, Notifications, Login, and Mana controls. Back follows the Nav store's explicit path before falling back to router history. Scoped deep rules keep the control strip compact across breakpoints. No data is fetched by those child controls here; the Header arranges light, spacing, and navigation without overwatering them.
